candidate_response:
(1) CURRENT STATUS
• Completed: Discovery phase, portfolio ROI assessment, and integrated 18-month roadmap deliverable submitted April 22.
• In-Flight: May 8 board pre-read preparation, implementation oversight scoping/negotiation, and Lumora (AI personalization vendor) contract dispute.
• Blocked/Delayed: POS modernization pause memo stalled by CIO Roberto Salazar, resulting in ~$280K/week continued burn. CDP termination facing formal legal protest from Lumora. Implementation oversight fee/scope not yet agreed upon.

(2) DIANE'S CONCERNS & WHY NOW
• Political Crossfire: Roberto is actively lobbying to pull implementation oversight in-house and resisting the roadmap's kill/pause recommendations. A board-level champion is also pushing to save the AI personalization initiative.
• Financial Bleed: The stalled pause memo is allowing POS spend to continue unchecked right before the board meeting.
• Vendor Risk: Lumora's formal protest creates legal/reputational exposure heading into the critical May 8 board session.
• Oversight Uncertainty: Procurement demands a decision on the post-engagement extension, but Diane refuses to commit without clear scope boundaries, fee justification, and a viable path to win internal approval against Roberto.
• Why Now: The roadmap was delivered, but operational friction, political resistance, and vendor pushback have converged simultaneously. She needs you back in the lead to defend the strategy at the board and lock in oversight terms.

(3) LIKELY OUTCOMES & YOUR STANCE
• Outcome A: Diane capitulates to internal pressure to keep AI personalization alive and fully reverse the POS pause. 
→ Stance: PUSH BACK. Protect the integrity of the ROI case. Propose a "phased decision gate" framework that preserves the kill recommendation while offering a conditional, time-boxed continuation path for the AI initiative.
• Outcome B: Diane wants to immediately sign a 6-month oversight extension at $180-220K/mo to guarantee execution support.
→ Stance: NEGOTIATE. Agree in principle to secure the board mandate, but restructure to a hybrid model (Meridian leads governance/milestone tracking; Helix ops handles day-to-day) to appease Roberto's in-house preference. Cap initial term at 3 months with clear KPI triggers for extension.
• Outcome C: Diane expects Meridian to absorb Lumora's legal fallout and unilaterally stop the POS burn.
→ Stance: CLARIFY BOUNDARIES. We provide strategic recommendation, negotiation leverage, and executive talking points, but procurement/legal owns contract execution. We will co-author the pause directive to ensure executive weight.

(4) SPECIFIC COMMITMENTS, ASKS & POSITIONS
• Core Position: "Strategic guardrails, not operational babysitting." Oversight covers governance, milestone tracking, and vendor performance management—not replacing the Helix PMO.
• Ask 1: Secure immediate co-signature on the POS pause directive from Diane and Roberto (or the CEO) to halt the $280K/week burn. Offer to facilitate the alignment call.
• Ask 2: Lock in the "Phase 2 Decision Gate" framing for AI personalization to neutralize the board champion's pushback while protecting the core recommendation.
• Commitment 1: Deliver a lean implementation oversight proposal by EOD Friday, structured as a 3-month pilot with transparent pricing and clear exit criteria.
• Commitment 2: Co-develop the May 8 board pre-read narrative, positioning Meridian's recommendations as de-risked execution steps with built-in financial safeguards, directly addressing Roberto's concerns through governance checkpoints rather than project ownership.

judge_opus.rationale: Solid coverage with good synthesis across threads, though it slightly mischaracterizes some details (e.g., the April 21 update already softened the POS framing in the deliverable; the 'capitulates' Outcome A is somewhat strawmanned given Diane has been protecting the recommendations). Could go deeper on the pre-existing 'phased decision' framing Marko already committed to in March.

judge_gpt.rationale: Accuracy is weakest because the response conflates the CDP termination with Lumora's AI personalization dispute, incorrectly states Diane refuses to commit on oversight, and overstates/introduces positions not clearly supported by the materials. Completeness is also limited because it omits several important completed and open items, including the cost savings memo, vendor contract review dependencies, and the softened POS recommendation actually adopted in the April 22 deliverable.

judge_minimax.rationale: Synthesis dimension is lowest — while the response links stakeholder incentives to positions, it misses key leverage points (Roberto's internally inconsistent CDP dependency claim) and the implicit connection between Lumora's legal exposure and implementation oversight negotiation positioning.
